title: Harness Lacing
desc: The training harnesses and practice plate straps are fraying again, which means the fitting racks need attention before anyone starts running drills in them.

steps.text:
The Barracks issue training harnesses built to survive more punishment than comfort. Chitin plates lace onto broad fiber straps, shoulder braces are swapped by size, and the rack labels are cut deep enough that even a half-tired quartermaster can still tell torso plates from flank guards at a glance. The system works right up until too many drill cycles wear the lacing thin and the whole rack starts looking one hard pull away from embarrassment.

The equipment keeper drops a bundle of fresh cord at your feet. "Replace the worst harness laces if you want the repair work," she says. "Or sort the spare plates back onto the correct pegs if you prefer the rack itself. Either way, I want the next fitting to involve less swearing."

options.label: Replace the worn lacing on the training harnesses
Sort the spare plates back onto the fitting pegs

steps.text:
The old laces come out stiff with salt and dust, polished smooth by too many hurried hands. You cut them free, thread fresh cord through the chitin eyelets, and draw each harness back to the tension marks burned into the leather straps. Once tightened properly, the plates stop sagging away from the frame and settle where they were meant to ride: close, even, and brutally practical.

The keeper tests one of the finished harnesses with a hard tug at both shoulders. Nothing slips. Nothing groans. The plates answer with the dry, satisfying click of proper fit. "Good," she says. "Training gear should fail only in the imagination."

options.label: Hang the repaired harnesses back on the rack

steps.text:
Spare plates migrate the way all equipment does when enough creatures are in a hurry: wrong peg, wrong size row, wrong lane entirely. You lift each piece in turn, read the score marks at the back edge, and return it to the fitting rack where it belongs. Thorax guards to the upper pegs, shoulder plates by weight, flank panels stacked where the drill crews can find them without turning the quarter room into a scavenging event.

By the time you finish, the rack looks like a system again instead of the aftermath of one. The keeper scans the neat rows and seems almost relieved by the absence of improvisation. "Better. Order is cheaper than replacement."

options.label: Step clear of the pegs and leave the rack in proper sequence
